1
erated Learning in
ecent Advances and
rends
Azadeh Pourkabirian, Wei Ni, Fellow, IEEE,
zgur B. Akan, Fellow, IEEE
amless,  low-latency,  and high-bandwidth connectivity
r users in immersive digital environments, such as the
etaverse [1]. By leveraging edge computing, CyberEdge
tworks process and store data closer to the user, reducing
tency and improving real-time interactions for applica-
ns in Augmented Reality (AR), Virtual Reality (VR),
d Mixed Reality (MR) [2]. The integration of IoT enables
namic data exchange between users’ devices, wearables,
d sensors,  further enhancing contextual awareness and
aptive resource management. This architecture ensures a
sponsive and scalable network infrastructure, supporting
e high computational and communication demands of
xt-generation connected experiences.
Protecting data privacy while addressing bandwidth lim-
tions is critical in CyberEdge networks, as IoT devices
cilitate  real-time,  immersive experiences with AR/VR
plications in the Metaverse [3]–[5]. These environments
nerate vast amounts of sensitive personal data, including
ometric information,  location, and interaction patterns,
aking them prime targets for cyber  threats and unau-
orized access [6]–[8]. Traditional cloud-based data pro-
ssing models require large-scale data transmission, which
t only increases privacy risks but also strains network
ndwidth,  resulting in latency issues  that degrade user
perience.
To protect data privacy while addressing bandwidth lim-
tions, federated learning (FL) is widely adopted in Cy-
rEdge networks as a privacy-preserving and bandwidth-
ficient machine learning paradigm [9]. As depicted in
g.  1,  instead of  uploading  raw data  to servers,  FL
ables local model  training on user devices, with only
odel  updates  (e.g., weight  adjustments)  being shared
ith the server or aggregated at the edge [10]–[12]. This
proach protects user privacy by keeping personal data
local devices while reducing bandwidth consumption,
significantly less data is transmitted compared to ma-
ine  learning  in  traditional  IoT systems. By enabling
stributed intelligence without centralized data collection,
enhances the scalability, responsiveness, and security of
yberEdge networks [13], [14], making them more efficient
r real-time, connected applications in the Metaverse.
With the rise of emerging cyber  threats, FL faces a
itical  resilience challenge,  including poisoning attacks,
versarial manipulations, model inversion, and Byzantine
tacks [15]–[17]. Since FL relies on distributed devices

an
to communication failures,  resource constraints, and data

heterogeneity, further affecting its reliability in real-world

deployments [19].  In CyberEdge networks, where FL is
th
expected to support real-time [20], privacy-sensitive appli-
R
cations in AR/VR, and the Metaverse [21], ensuring its
in
resilience is paramount. A compromised FL system not
an
only threatens user privacy and security but also under-
an
mines the effectiveness of intelligent services that depend
to
on it  [22].

metadata.section_title: Introduction of Federated Learning in CyberEdge Networks

text:
Strengthening FL’s resilience through robust
ad
aggregation mechanisms, anomaly detection, secure com-
de
munication, and trust-aware learning frameworks is crucial
for maintaining the integrity, availability, and security of
L
CyberEdge networks [2], [23].
do
tu
B. Our Motivation
on
In this paper, we investigate two emerging key tech-  co
niques  towards resilient FL (ResFL)  in CyberEdge net-  gu
works, i.e., joint training and agglomerative deduction, as  do
well as feature-oriented threats and defenses. Specifically,  fr
joint  training and agglomerative deduction can enhance  tr
FL resilience by leveraging heterogeneous data and hi-  an
erarchical aggregation with fault  tolerance and anomaly  pr
detection. Heterogeneous data, a fundamental challenge in  an
FL, stems from diverse edge devices with non-independent  tr
and identically distributed (non-IID) data distributions, re-  en
quiring adaptive aggregation strategies, such as hierarchical  pl
learning, where local models are first aggregated at inter-
mediate levels before contributing to the global model. This
C
approach improves scalability and reduces communication
overhead. Meanwhile,  fault  tolerance mechanisms ensure
system robustness by detecting and mitigating the impact
of unreliable or compromised devices  through anomaly
detection techniques,  such as statistical analysis or ma-
chine learning-based outlier detection. An agglomerative

2
duction can  further  improve  resilience by  iteratively
fining updates, filtering out low-quality contributions, and
ioritizing reliable data sources,  leading to more stable
odel convergence.
On the other hand, feature-oriented threats and defenses
cus on securing FL models against adversarial manipu-
tions, particularly poisoning attacks as well as inference
d reconstruction attacks. In poisoning attacks, adversaries
ject malicious data or manipulate local model updates to
grade global model performance. Defenses against such
reats include robust aggregation techniques (e.g., Krum,
edian-based aggregation) and anomaly detection mech-
isms  that  identify suspicious updates based on model
vergence.  Inference and reconstruction attacks exploit
odel updates  to infer sensitive training data or  recon-
ruct private features, threatening data confidentiality. De-
nse strategies, such as differential privacy, homomorphic
cryption, and secure multi-party computation, mitigate
ese risks by obfuscating updates and limiting information
akage. Addressing both poisoning and inference threats,
ature-oriented security mechanisms can enhance the ro-
stness and privacy of ResFL in CyberEdge networks.
Furthermore, we  investigate several key opportunities
d future research directions for constructing ResFL in
yberEdge networks. The evolution of 6G brings ultra-low
tency, massive connectivity, and AI-native infrastructure
at can significantly accelerate the deployment of ResFL.
esearch can focus on optimizing FL for 6G by leveraging
telligent  resource allocation,  semantic communication,
d dynamic edge-cloud collaboration. Network-aware FL
d federated reinforcement  learning can be developed
support  self-optimizing systems capable of  real-time
aptation to network conditions, mobility,  and security
mands, enhancing overall resilience and performance.
Another promising direction lies  in integrating Large
anguage Models (LLMs) and enabling collaborative cross-
main and cross-silo ResFL.  LLMs  introduce oppor-
nities for privacy-preserving and decentralized training
edge devices,  especially when enhanced with model
mpression, personalized FL, and secure protocols  that
ard against adversarial threats. At the same time, cross-
main collaboration supported by interoperable learning
ameworks  allows diverse  sectors,  such as healthcare,
ansportation,  and smart  infrastructure,  to contribute  to
d benefit from shared intelligence while preserving data
ivacy and regulatory compliance. These future directions
d insights pave the way for building robust, scalable, and
ustworthy ResFL systems that can adapt to heterogeneous
vironments and empower next-generation CyberEdge ap-
ications.
. Contributions
The key contributions of this paper are as follows:
• We study the joint training and agglomerative deduc-
tion techniques  in CyberEdge networks, which aim
to improve ResFL by leveraging heterogeneous data,
hierarchical aggregation, fault tolerance, and anomaly.

ra
In this section, we review the literature thoroughly in
ni
terms of the reliability and security of FL in CyberEdge
up
networks.
FL
pr
A. Reliable Federated Learning
da
Recent  advancements  in FL have  led to diverse ap-  of
proaches to enhance the reliability and robustness of dis-  pr
tributed systems, particularly when integrated with edge
computing and IoT applications. For instance, a federated  ne
edge architecture incorporating semantic IoT was studied  W
by Li et al. [24], enabling AR/VR users to offload resource-  in
intensive semantic processing tasks to edge servers. These  sy

3
rvers collaboratively train a unified semantic model using
-based frameworks. To ensure reliability and efficiency,
e researchers developed a dynamic sequential-to-parallel
approach, incorporating semantic compression and com-
nsation techniques. This strategy can merge compressed
storical semantic data and fine-tune classifier parameters,
us optimizing resource usage and model accuracy.
Addressing FL reliability from a security perspective,
urmu et al.  [25]  introduced a customized,  inequality-
are FL designed specifically for secure color image trans-
ission within CyberEdge networks. Their personalized
proach adapts data sampling algorithms to client-specific
quirements based on the local availability of labeled data.
omplementing  these personalized FL efforts, Kang et
. [26] studied a reputation-based metric to select trusted
orkers. By leveraging participants’ reputation values, their
amework filters unreliable clients, enhancing the accuracy
d trustworthiness of the learning process.
Several  surveys have broadened the understanding of
liable FL and identified critical challenges across various
ctors. Nguyen et al. [27] presented an extensive survey
phasizing reliable FL’s applications in smart healthcare,
cluding federated electronic health records management,
mote health monitoring, medical imaging, and COVID-
detection. Their work outlined motivations,  technical
erequisites, and opportunities for further deployment  in
althcare systems. In addition, Huang et al. [28] provided a
view of FL techniques focused on three aspects: general-
ation, robustness, and fairness. They categorized existing
ethods based on distinct  task settings,  such as cross-
ient and out-client shifts in generalizable FL, Byzantine
tacks, reward conflicts, and prediction biases. Their work
so highlighted data heterogeneity as an ongoing critical
allenge that demands targeted future research.
Meanwhile, Gabrielli et al.  [29] and Jiang et al.  [30]
fered another perspective by categorizing reliable FL
hemes  into two primary groups:  traditional distributed
mputing-based FL and blockchain-integrated FL. Their
alyses identified significant challenges, including vulner-
ilities to adversarial attacks and the absence of effective
centive mechanisms to encourage participation.
Complementing these insights, Khan et al. [31] evaluated
specifically tailored for IoT applications,  focusing on
ucial metrics like scalability, quantization, and security.
heir survey provided a taxonomy addressing system pa-
meters, federated optimization schemes, incentive mecha-
sms, security measures, and operational modes. Building
on this, Boobalan et al. [32] reviewed the integration of
with industrial IoT, discussing critical aspects such as
ivacy preservation,  resource management, and efficient
ta handling. They discussed the motivations and benefits
combining FL with industrial IoT, emphasizing privacy
otection and enabling on-device learning capabilities.
Furthermore, the structural considerations and impacts of
twork topologies on FL effectiveness were explored by
u et al. [33], who revealed that certain network topologies
troduce additional constraints and opportunities  in FL
stems. For example, employing ring topology can signif-

This section investigates  joint  training and agglomer-
ive deduction, which leverages heterogeneous data and
erarchical aggregation with fault tolerance and anomaly
tection to improve FL resilience.
. Heterogeneous Data and Hierarchical Aggregation
FL inherently operates with heterogeneous data due to
e diversity of clients, fluctuating network conditions, and
rying application requirements [46]–[48]. Addressing this
terogeneity is critical  to maintaining the robustness of
stributed nodes and ensuring efficient model training [12].
can explore hierarchical  aggregation as an effective
rategy to mitigate the negative impacts of data hetero-
neity, thereby preserving model robustness and accuracy.
Data heterogeneity typically manifests  in two distinct
rms: non-independent and identically distributed (non-
D) data, where the client’s local data distributions vary
gnificantly; and system heterogeneity, where participat-
g nodes differ considerably in computational capabilities
d communication resources. These factors can adversely
fect FL by reducing convergence speed and degrading
erall performance [42], [49]. Traditional FL aggregation
ethods, such as FedAvg, often fail  to handle heteroge-
ous data effectively, necessitating the development of
ore adaptive and robust aggregation strategies [45].
Hierarchical aggregation addresses these challenges by
ganizing clients  into subgroups based on factors such
statistical similarity, geographical proximity, or compu-
tional capacity. Local models within these clusters are
gregated first, forming an intermediate aggregation layer
fore global model updates. This additional aggregation
ep  reduces  the  impact  of  extreme model  divergence
used by non-IID data distributions [50].
Recent studies confirm that multi-tier FL architectures
gnificantly enhance robustness against Byzantine failures
d slow-converging clients [41]. For instance, as shown
Fig. 2, You et al. [41] introduced a method of gradient
scheduling that enhances convergence rates and model
ability in scenarios with heterogeneous data by arranging
e order of gradient updates. Specifically,  their approach
oups clients based on similarities in label distributions,
bsequently reassigning client identities according to these
usters. From these grouped gradients, representative sam-
es can be selected to form an IID gradient batch, pro-
ding optimizers with accurate momentum estimates for
proved training effectiveness.
Moreover,  personalized FL (PFL)  techniques address
ta heterogeneity by customizing local models for indi-
dual  clients while  leveraging global  insights. Chen et
. [42] introduced retrogress-resilient FL methods tailored
handle imbalanced data distributions commonly found in

bo

erogeneity to improve overall convergence. Similarly, Zuo
ag
et al. [43] presented a Byzantine-resilient FL strategy in-
ba
corporating adaptive weighting mechanisms that effectively
ef
mitigate the influence of malicious clients and enhance
in
system resilience.
C
CyberEdge networks, which combine edge computing
tio
with cyber-physical systems, particularly benefit from hi-
st
erarchical FL strategies. For  instance, Zhou et  al.  [40]
ad
described a decentralized peer-to-peer FL framework for
mobile robotic systems, enhancing privacy and resilience
through secure,  reputation-based virtual clustering. Simi-  B
larly, Mukherjee et al.

[45] utilized vertical federated rein-
forcement learning (FedSAC) to optimize energy distribu-  an
tion in smart grids while ensuring robustness against cyber  ad
threats,  demonstrating its practical  applicability through  se
hardware-in-the-loop simulations.  ag
Hierarchical aggregation thus emerges as a promising  m
solution to the challenges posed by data heterogeneity in
FL. Future research directions  include: dynamic cluster  m

rmation to adapt aggregation hierarchies based on real-
e data analytics; hybrid aggregation methods combining
ulti-tier aggregation and reinforcement learning for opti-

yzantine-resilient  aggregation mechanisms  suitable  for
versarial environments. Addressing these challenges will
gnificantly enhance the resilience and adaptability of FL
CyberEdge networks.
Table I provides a comprehensive comparison of ex-
ting heterogeneous data handling techniques and hierar-
ical aggregation approaches  in ResFL.  In general, FL
counters data heterogeneity stemming from non-IID local
stributions and varying node capabilities. This diversity
pedes  convergence,  lowers model  accuracy,  and de-
ands more robust aggregation methods than conventional
proaches like FedAvg. Hierarchical aggregation, which
oups clients by factors such as statistical similarity or
mputing capacity, emerges as a key strategy. Techniques
e gradient rescheduling and adaptive weighting further
lster FL’s  resilience  to Byzantine  failures,  stragglers,
d malicious clients. PFL complements these efforts by
iloring local models while capitalizing on global insights,
ereby improving performance under disparate data con-
tions.  In CyberEdge networks,  integrating hierarchical
gregation with novel security measures (e.g., reputation-
sed clustering, Byzantine  resilience,  etc.) has proven
fective for privacy, reliability, and resource optimization
real-world scenarios like mobile robotics and smart grids.
ontinued work on adaptive clustering, hybrid aggrega-
n, and advanced security frameworks is anticipated to
rengthen FL’s robustness and adaptability in dynamic or
versarial environments.
. Fault Tolerance and Anomaly Detection
In FL,  it  is  important  to ensure  fault  tolerance and
omaly detection to maintain the model’s reliability in
versarial  and resource-constrained environments. This
ction explores various strategies to enhance FL resilience
ainst Byzantine attacks,  communication  failures,  and
alicious data manipulation.
Byzantine failures occur when malicious or compro-
ised clients provide incorrect model updates, potentially.

This section studies the feature-oriented threats and de-
nse strategies, which are designed to secure FL models
ainst  adversarial manipulations,  particularly poisoning
tacks, as well as inference and reconstruction attacks.

TABLE II: Key Techniques for Fault Tolera

learning [59]  comple
A. Feature Extraction with Poisoning Attacks
1) Threat Models: Model poisoning attacks are a signif-
icant threat to FL systems, where adversaries leverage com-  B
promised or malicious clients to submit manipulated local
updates, intentionally steering the global model away from
its correct learning trajectory [60]. The primary objective
of these attacks is to mislead the learning outcome, thereby  Fi
degrading the accuracy, reliability, and trustworthiness of  in
FL-based decision-making.  w
Several  recent  studies have expanded  the scope and
sophistication of model poisoning attacks. For  instance,
Li  et  al.  [61]  introduced the Adversarial Graph Atten-  a
tion Network (AGAT), an advanced adversarial framework  in
specifically designed to launch fairness attacks by strate-  m
gically manipulating FL training processes. AGAT maxi-  m
mizes the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between user-  no
submitted updates and the global model, utilizing a Graph  to
Autoencoder  (GAE)  trained via sub-gradient descent  to  m
reconstruct correlations among benign model updates. This
strategy increases reconstruction loss, ensuring malicious  al
updates remain indistinguishable from genuine contribu-  vo
tions, thereby complicating attack detection.  m
As shown in Fig. 4, a new “training-data-untethered” poi-  m
soning strategy was proposed by Li et al. [62], [63], which  su
uses adversarial variational graph autoencoders  to craft  ca
malicious models from benign local updates alone, without  its
direct access to training data. By extracting graph structural
correlations and adversarially reconstructing these correla-  W
tions, the resulting malicious local models become highly  w
effective and particularly challenging  to detect,  further  at
amplifying threats to FL integrity.  in
Cao et al. [64] developed a distinct approach through a  tr
fake-client-based model poisoning attack, where an adver-
sary injects artificially created clients into the FL environ-  ce
ment. These fake clients submit deliberately manipulated  m
updates that push the global model towards an adversari-  by
ally chosen suboptimal baseline, compromising FL system  cl
accuracy.  st
Two poisoning techniques, i.e., label-flipping and model-  st
update poisoning, were systematically examined by Thein  se
et  al.  [65]  to evaluate  their detrimental  impact on FL-
based intrusion detection systems. The study pointed out  fo

or system faults

cross-validation and  low-  ditional  coordination, which may

g malicious models  from benign local updates alone,
ithout direct access to training data.
critical weakness: As  the heterogeneity of user data
creases,  robust aggregation methods fail  to effectively
itigate poisoned contributions, causing significant perfor-
ance degradation. Supporting this finding, Abou et al. [66]
ted that data heterogeneity further exacerbates challenges
global model  convergence, making FL systems even
ore vulnerable to poisoning attacks.
Expanding on traditional poisoning methods, Yang et
. [67] introduced a model shuffle poisoning attack that in-
lves strategically shuffling and scaling parameters within
alicious models.

Unlike conventional  approaches,  this
ethod preserves benign appearances and test accuracy,
btly disrupting global model convergence. As a result, it
n slow down learning or lead to divergence, complicating
identification and mitigation.
Focused specifically on FL in autonomous vehicles,
ang et al. [68] designed a dynamic data poisoning frame-
ork leveraging a bandit-based approach. Their black-box
tack adaptively selects vulnerable regions within the steer-
g angle regression task, increasing effectiveness across FL
aining rounds while evading detection mechanisms.
In addition, backdoor poisoning remains an ongoing con-
rn, as presented by Lyu et al. [69]. Their approach enables
alicious actors to insert covert  triggers into FL models
coordinating model updates from multiple compromised
ients. Specifically designed to bypass common defense
rategies,  this backdoor attack presents a persistent and
ealthy threat capable of severely undermining FL system
curity.
2) Defense Strategies: Recent research has increasingly
cused on developing defense mechanisms  to counter

sophisticated model poisoning attacks in FL. Zhang et al.
introduced FedCAMAE [70], a new defense approach that
leverages visual explanation techniques to enhance detec-
tion capabilities beyond conventional Euclidean distance-
based or machine  learning-based methods.  Specifically,
FedCAMAE integrates Layer Class Activation Mapping
(LayerCAM) with an autoencoder to produce detailed heat
maps for each local model update submitted to the central
server. These heat maps serve as fine-grained visual rep-
resentations, which the autoencoder further refines, high-
lighting hidden features and improving distinguishability
between benign and malicious updates.
Extending visual  explanation-based defenses,  another
promising approach was proposed by Zheng et al.  [71].
Fi
As shown in Fig. 5,  their framework combines Gradient-
C
weighted Class Activation Mapping  (GradCAM)  and
autoencoders,  effectively  improving detection  accuracy
against model poisoning attacks. By analyzing anomalous
en
heat maps generated through GradCAM,  this method can
pr
strengthen FL security, enhancing the ability to identify and
he
isolate malicious updates.
po
Many existing defenses often assume independent and
fe
identically distributed (IID) data environments, resulting in
co
reduced performance when confronted with non-IID data.
pl
To address these limitations, Chen et al.  [72] developed
a defense mechanism specifically designed for non-IID
of
scenarios. Their method employs representational similarity
analysis to systematically evaluate the alignment between
global and local models. By constructing a representational  B
consistency set and applying clustering algorithms such as
k-means,  the framework effectively identifies and isolates  si
adversarial entities,  improving defense robustness in het-  m
erogeneous data settings.
Complementing these visual  and clustering-based de-  is
fenses, Panda et al. [73] introduced SparseFed, a technique  co
that mitigates model poisoning attacks  through gradient  to
clipping and  top-k sparsification. During each  training  Je
round, only the top-k gradients with the highest magnitude  th
are aggregated and used to update the global model. Since  ex
attackers often manipulate gradients in directions divergent  si
from benign updates,  their malicious  contributions  are  an
inherently minimized or excluded.  en
A two-phase approach for detecting malicious updates  ge
was further extended in [74].  In the first phase, kernel  tr
density estimation evaluates  the  relative distribution of  en
local model updates, identifying anomalous patterns. In the
second phase, a statistical detection threshold differentiates  tr
malicious from benign updates. This structured analysis  fr
enhances the precision of identifying compromised users,  an
significantly strengthening FL resilience.  de
Cao et  al.  [75]  expanded  the defense  landscape by  de
designing an ensemble-based FL framework. Their strategy  ys
partitions users into multiple groups,  training a separate  fo
global model  for each group independently. A majority  in
voting mechanism then aggregates predictions from these
models, substantially enhancing FL robustness and mini-  en
mizing the impact of adversarial manipulations.  tr
Focusing specifically on FL applications within  IoT  da

vironments, Zhang et al. [76] introduced a logits-based
edictive model deployed at the server level. This model
lps identify and trace incoming logits, effectively pin-
inting potentially malicious sources.

Concurrently,  the
derated model undergoes adversarial training, proactively
unteracting attacker manipulations and substantially com-
icating stealthy poisoning attacks.
Table III describes the critical representative techniques
feature-oriented threats and defense strategies.
. Feature-based Inference and Reconstruction Attacks
Feature-based inference and reconstruction attacks pose
gnificant threats to FL, as adversaries can exploit shared
odel updates to infer sensitive information.
1) Threat Models: One significant threat to FL systems
the Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based re-

Given the evolving sophistication of GAN-based and
her reconstruction attacks, it is critical to develop robust
untermeasures. Strengthening differential privacy tech-
ques, improving secure aggregation protocols, and incor-
rating adversarial  training are key areas for enhancing
’s resistance to inference attacks, thus safeguarding data
ivacy in federated systems.
2) Defense Strategies:  To counter  the  rising  threat
feature-based inference and reconstruction attacks,  re-
archers have developed various defensive strategies aimed
preserving privacy and enhancing the robustness of FL
stems. Zhao et al. [84] introduced a private aggregation
heme that can strengthen FL systems against  inference
tacks. As  shown  in Fig.  7,  their  approach  leverages
vanced encryption techniques,  securing model updates
fectively without compromising computational efficiency.
omplementing this, Lee et al.  [86] explored defensive
ural networks employing adversarial perturbation tech-
ques designed to obfuscate gradients,  thus making data
construction substantially more difficult for adversaries.
Further advancing privacy protections, Xu et al.  [89]
tegrated differential privacy mechanisms directly into FL
odels, reducing vulnerability to client-side data inference
tacks. Their approach demonstrates how robust modeling
actices can limit  adversarial  inference capabilities.  In.

A. 6G-Assisted ResFL
ro
One of  the primary challenges  in 6G-assisted ResFL  R
is achieving an optimal trade-off between communication  se
efficiency and model training performance. While 6G offers  th
ultra-low latency and high-speed data transmission to Cy-  in
berEdge networks, FL models, especially large-scale deep  on
learning models,  still  require significant  communication  na
resources for frequent parameter exchanges between edge  be
devices and servers [95]–[97]. The challenge intensifies  fu

Future work needs to develop adaptive communication
rategies such as event-triggered updates,  sparsification,
antization, and hierarchical aggregation to reduce trans-
ission overhead while maintaining model accuracy and
bustness. Moreover, integrating semantic communication
FL, where only the most informative features are trans-
itted  rather  than  raw updates,  could  further  enhance
ficiency in 6G environments.
Another  challenge  in 6G-assisted ResFL is  the new
anagement of heterogeneous data distributions and the
sign of  scalable hierarchical  aggregations  to improve
arning efficiency as well  as  resilience.  In CyberEdge
tworks, edge devices generate diverse data types with
rying quality,  availability,  and statistical distributions,
aking it difficult to achieve global model generalization
hile maintaining local  adaptability. 6G’s AI-native  in-
astructure can enable intelligent data clustering, adaptive
gregation, and cross-layer coordination [98]–[100], but
timally selecting aggregation points and balancing local
rsus global updates remain open problems.
As a next-step direction,  it  is critical  to explore dy-
mic hierarchical  aggregation mechanisms  that  can be
justed based on network conditions, device reliability,
d data distribution patterns. In addition, integrating fed-
ated meta-learning and transfer learning can help ResFL
odels quickly adapt to new environments and unseen data
stributions while reducing computational  and commu-
cation burdens in large-scale, hierarchical 6G-supported
yberEdge networks.
. Joint Training of LLMs and ResFL
LLMs can enable privacy-preserving and decentralized
aining of ResFL across edge devices while maintaining
bustness [101]–[103]. However,  integrating LLMs with
esFL in CyberEdge networks can introduce considerable
curity risks, particularly in defending against adversarial
reats,  such as model poisoning, backdoor attacks, and
ference attacks. Since LLMs require extensive training
diverse datasets, attackers can exploit  their federated
ture by injecting malicious updates, subtly altering model
havior, or embedding hidden backdoors that trigger harm-
l outputs under specific conditions. Unlike conventional

Fig. 8: Research opportunities for developing
FL models, LLMs are more susceptible to memorization  E
and prompt-based vulnerabilities, increasing the risk of data  R
leakage even in privacy-preserving settings.  po
Future research is required to focus on robust defense  H
mechanisms, including adversarial training, anomaly detec-  ov
tion, and secure aggregation techniques, to identify, isolate,  in
and mitigate adversarial influences in ResFL deployments.  re
On the other hand, maintaining trustworthiness in the
joint  training of LLMs and ResFL is challenging due  er
to  the heterogeneous and decentralized nature of  edge  sy
devices, each contributing to updates that may vary in qual-  an
ity,  reliability, or intent [104]–[106].  In particular, LLMs  tr
require complex semantic understanding, making  them  th
prone to biased learning,  inconsistent generalization, and  fu
unreliable knowledge aggregation in diverse CyberEdge  in
networks [102],  [107],  [108].

Furthermore, verifying the  di
integrity of  local updates and preventing misinformation  fr
propagation become critical issues, especially when LLMs  sh
are applied in sensitive applications, such as autonomous  hi
systems, healthcare, and finance.  pr
More efforts are needed to develop trust-aware ResFL  B
frameworks,  integrating advanced  techniques,  including  ac
blockchain-based verification, reputation-based client scor-  an
ing, and incentive-driven participation mechanisms, to en-
sure  fair  and reliable model  contributions.  In addition,
te
self-assessment strategies within LLMs can be explored to
tr
evaluate their own responses for potential biases or hallu-

cinations, enhancing overall trust in ResFL-based decision-

making systems.

C. Cross-Domain and Cross-Silo ResFL  gl
Collaborative  cross-domain  and  cross-silo  ResFL  ad
presents a critical  opportunity for  enhancing resilience  an
in future CyberEdge networks, especially across sectors,  si
such  as  healthcare,  autonomous  vehicles,  finance,  in
and  smart  cities  [109]–[113].  In  these  contexts,  data  do
is  inherently  fragmented  across  various  entities  or  si
domains,  such as  hospitals,  automotive manufacturers,  st
or  municipal  infrastructures,  where  each  maintains
distinct data characteristics and strict privacy constraints.  re

11
future ResFL in CyberEdge networks.
nabling  these  organizations  to  collaboratively  train
esFL models without  sharing  raw data  can unlock
werful  intelligence while preserving data sovereignty.
owever,  such collaboration is non-trivial,  as  it has  to
ercome challenges related to data heterogeneity, system
teroperability, trust, and compliance with domain-specific
gulations.
A major research direction is the development of interop-
able learning frameworks that allow cross-domain ResFL
stems with differing data modalities, model architectures,
d system capabilities to participate effectively in joint
aining. This involves designing flexible ResFL protocols
at support asynchronous updates, heterogeneous model
sion, and hybrid aggregation strategies adaptable to vary-
g data formats and tasks (e.g., combining image-based
agnostics  from healthcare with numerical  sensor data
om vehicular networks)  [114]–[117]. Such frameworks
ould also account for resource diversity, enabling both
gh-end servers and lightweight edge devices to contribute
oportionally without  compromising  the global model.
uilding standardized APIs and modular ResFL interfaces
ross platforms will be essential for seamless integration
d deployment at scale.
Another key direction lies in robust domain adaptation
chniques  tailored for ResFL settings. Since data dis-
ibutions often differ significantly across domains (non-
D data)  [118],  [119],  global models  trained via con-
ntional FL may suffer from poor generalization. Future
search can investigate domain-invariant feature extraction,
rsonalized ResFL, and meta-learning methods to allow
obal models to learn from cross-domain knowledge while
apting to local nuances. Moreover, hierarchical ResFL
d clustered ResFL approaches can be leveraged to group
milar domains before federating at a higher level, improv-
g convergence speed and performance while preserving
main-specific insights. These solutions should also be re-
lient to domain shifts and adversarial conditions, ensuring
able performance in real-world dynamic environments.
In  addition,  the  design  of  privacy-preserving  and
gulation-compliant protocols is essential for trusted cross-.

[
This survey focused on ResFL in CyberEdge networks,
which is a rapidly evolving field that demands novel ap-
proaches to enhance security, efficiency, and adaptability.
[
We explored feature-oriented threats,  such as poisoning,
inference, and reconstruction attacks, which remain crit-
ical,  requiring continuous advancements  in anomaly de-
[
tection and resilient aggregation techniques. We investi-
gated adaptive hierarchical  learning and fault  tolerance
[
mechanisms  that  play a crucial  role  in mitigating  the
challenges posed by non-IID data and unreliable devices,
ensuring stable convergence and efficient communication.
[
For future opportunities, the incorporation of 6G and LLMs
offers significant potential for improving decentralized and
privacy-preserving learning,  leveraging ultra-low latency,
massive connectivity, and AI-driven optimization. ResFL  [
can also pave the way for robust cross-domain and cross-
silo edge intelligence, such as autonomous systems, health-  [
care, and smart cities, where data privacy and resilience
are paramount. As future research unfolds, interdisciplinary
collaboration among security, networking, and AI commu-  [
nities will be a key to realizing the full potential of ResFL
and driving its real-world deployment.
